cataloging_subject.label: The Wilton Chronicle
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English verse work composed for or in close association with the Benedictine convent at Wilton around 1420. It joins a history of Wilton and its royal founders to the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith of Wilton. The sole witness is British Library Cotton MS Faustina B III, current ff. 199r-263r. CME00147 represents Carl Horstmann's 1883 edition of the 4,986 surviving verse lines and also preserves the complete adjacent Latin Nomina fundatorum plus the first seven of fourteen Auctoritates entries in the same manuscript hand. Those companion representation parts and Horstmann's German editorial frame do not redefine the abstract work as mixed-form or German-language content.

catalog_summary.title: The Wilton Chronicle
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Wilton poet
catalog_summary.date: Around 1420; sole surviving witness from the first quarter of the fifteenth century; first published in 1830
catalog_summary.region: Wilton, Wiltshire, England; an inferred South Wiltshire original is transmitted in a mixed-language copy made by a scribe associated with the West Midlands

scope.description: The Middle English verse work.

assertion.notes: The author had close knowledge of Wilton and probably wrote for its female religious community, but no personal name or secure clerical status is known.

notes: Older suggestions that the poet was one of Wilton's chaplains or that the sole copy was autograph remain hypotheses, not established authorship.
Benskin distinguishes authorial language from the extant copyist's mixed dialect and rejects a simple autograph model.

subject_statements.label: Translation of relics and shrine devotion

scope.description: Edith's incorrupt body, elevation, shrine, relic protection, and later cult.
Royal succession, West Saxon history, Norman rule, and Wilton's place in English history.

summaries.value: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within a long account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, its royal patrons, and the abbey's institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, miracles associated with her shrine and relics, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its community. Its combination of hagiography and history constructs a usable past for a female religious house rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.

scope.description: The abstract Middle English work.

summaries.value: The poem opens with English and West Saxon history and Wilton's founders, moves into Edgar's encounter with Wulfthryth, and then loses an entire twelve-folio gathering after encoded line 1030. The surviving text resumes with Edith's upbringing, ascetic life, miracles, death, and first shrine cult. At line 2391 Horstmann marks a second part beginning with the translation of Edith's relics; later sections narrate Wulfthryth, royal patrons, punishments, healings, visions, and Edith's continuing protection of Wilton. Two Latin prose texts follow in the same manuscript hand: a list of Wilton founders through Henry V and a fourteen-entry list of authorities. CME00147 includes the founders text and only the first seven authority entries, ending on printed p. 112 / current f. 264r.

scope.description: Surviving work, complete Nomina fundatorum, and the opening half of Auctoritates represented by CME00147.

summaries.value: CME00147's root language attribute lists Middle English, Latin, and German because the encoding preserves the poem, a Latin companion selection, and Horstmann's German apparatus. Work-level form remains verse and its primary language remains Middle English. Horstmann's long German title belongs to the 1883 edition. Current British Library foliation is five leaves later than Horstmann's old milestones across this component: the poem is now ff. 199r-263r rather than ff. 194r-258r. The same current f. 263r and IIIF canvas 535 contain the poem's ending above and Nomina fundatorum below; the encoded selection ends on f. 264r / canvas 537 before the Auctoritates continuation on f. 264v / canvas 538.

scope.description: Cataloging boundary among work, appendices, manuscript, edition, and encoding.

extent.description: One surviving verse work represented by 4,986 L elements in three LG wrappers. The wrappers reflect Horstmann/XML segmentation around a twelve-folio manuscript lacuna after line 1030 and a marked second part at line 2391; they are not three independent works. The CME item additionally contains the complete Latin Nomina fundatorum in 33 P elements and the opening Auctoritates paragraph with a seven-item LIST; seven further authorities on source p. 113 are omitted. Across TEXT it has 3 DIV1, 5 DIV2, 6 HEAD, 46 P, 113 PB, 4,986 L, 3 LG, 347 NOTE, 65 MILESTONE, 6,738 italic HI, 396 SUP, and 2 TAILNOTE elements.

content_parts.label: The Wilton Chronicle
content_parts.summary: The complete surviving Middle English verse work: Wilton's institutional and royal history integrated with the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith.

locators.value: TEXT/BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; three DIV2 and LG wrappers; 4,986 L elements

notes: The poem and first Latin appendix share current f. 263r / IIIF canvas 535; the verse ends above the appendix opening.

content_parts.label: Historical opening and transition to Edith's family
content_parts.summary: English and West Saxon history, Wilton's foundations and royal benefactors, and the narrative transition to Edgar's encounter with Wulfthryth; the surviving section breaks at the manuscript's twelve-folio lacuna.

locators.value: 1-1030, ending at the lacuna after printed p. 24

notes: Horstmann estimates that the removed gathering contained about 980 lines concerning Edgar and Wulfthryth, Edith's birth, and her upbringing. That estimate is not counted as surviving extent.

content_parts.label: Edith's life, death, and first miracles
content_parts.summary: The poem resumes after the lost gathering with Edith's religious upbringing, ascetic devotion, royal and abbatial setting, miracles, death, burial, and early shrine cult.

locators.value: surviving continuation after line 1030 through line 2390

content_parts.label: Translation of Edith and later miracles
content_parts.summary: Edith's relic translation and incorrupt body, miracles involving the shrine, Wulfthryth, royal patrons and wrongdoers, healings, visions, and Edith's continuing protection of Wilton.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']/DIV2[3]/LG; HEAD 'Hic incipit translacio sce Edithe'

notes: Horstmann calls this Part II, but his note says the manuscript itself continues without a paragraph break or initial; the heading is marginal.

content_parts.label: Nomina fundatorum
content_parts.summary: A Latin prose account and list of founders and royal benefactors of Wilton, ending with Henry V as the reigning king and providing evidence for composition around 1420.

locators.value: ff. 263r-264r, beginning below the poem on f. 263r and ending above Auctoritates on f. 264r

notes: The appendix is substantive manuscript content, not Horstmann's modern prose, but its exact work-level status relative to the poem is kept distinct.

content_parts.label: Auctoritates
content_parts.summary: The encoded opening of a fourteen-entry Latin source list, naming the Legenda sanctae Edithae, Henry Crompe, Bede, the Cistercian Chronicle, Marianus Scotus, William of Malmesbury, and Henry of Huntingdon. The remaining seven entries are present in Horstmann and the manuscript but absent from CME00147.

locators.value: encoded opening on f. 264r; unencoded continuation on f. 264v
encoded opening on p. 112; unencoded continuation on p. 113
BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='addendum']/DIV2[@N='2']; one P containing one LIST with seven ITEM elements

notes: The full source list documents fourteen named authorities; the XML retains only the first seven and does not identify the exact physical exemplars or recensions used.
item_count 0 keeps this ancillary list from being counted as seven separately cataloged works.

content_parts.label: Horstmann's 1883 editorial frame and apparatus
content_parts.summary: The modern German title page, supplied headings, footnotes, marginal source labels, lacuna notes, punctuation, parenthetical restorations, italic typography, and folio and page milestones represented in CME00147.

locators.value: title page and printed pp. 1-112; introduction pp. III-VIII, Auctoritates continuation p. 113, and remarks/Appendix II pp. 114-116 omitted
FRONT title page plus BODY HEAD, HEADNOTE, TAILNOTE, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, HI, SUP, and parenthetical layers

notes: The XML contains 6,738 italic HI spans. Many mark expanded or supplied letter sequences, while some participate in readings and corrections; no single interpretation should be projected onto every italic span without image-by-image collation.
This representation part is excluded from the medieval work's one-item extent and intrinsic language/form classification.

related_works.title: Legenda sanctae Edithae / Vita et translatio S. Edithae
notes: Modern scholarship treats the eleventh-century Edith legend associated with Goscelin as a principal source for the hagiographic material.
Horstmann doubted that the short twelve-chapter Bollandist Vita was identical to the Legenda named by the poet. The exact recension and physical exemplar therefore remain unresolved.

related_works.title: The Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 265r-279v

notes: The companion Middle English verse life follows the Latin Wilton appendices in the same manuscript component and is generally assigned to the same anonymous author.
Benskin confirms close linguistic and scribal association while keeping author and copyist distinct.

evidence.citation: CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00147.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: TEXT title page, poem, addendum, notes, folio milestones, and page milestones
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for file identity, the complete encoded text, languages, title, incipit, explicit, work and appendix boundaries, lacuna, line numbering, source labels, and editorial typography.

evidence.citation: Local XPath structural audit of CME00147.xml.
evidence.summary: Counts 3 DIV1, 5 DIV2, 6 HEAD, 46 P, 113 PB, 4,986 L, 3 LG, 347 NOTE, 65 MILESTONE, 7 ITEM, 1 LIST, 6,738 italic HI, 396 SUP, and 2 TAILNOTE elements. The three poem DIV2/LG segments contain 1,030, 1,361, and 2,595 L elements; Nomina fundatorum contains 33 P elements and the Auctoritates opening contains one P with seven ITEM elements. The last PB is printed p. 112, so the seven additional authorities on p. 113 are outside the XML.

Binds the descriptive work record to the repository artifact, U-M encoding, Horstmann and Black editions, sole Cotton witness, complete manuscript and edition facsimiles, DIMEV record, and modern scholarship.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883).

notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached PDF, not a selected extract.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199-280, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record 041-001103881.
evidence.locator: ff. 199r-263r Life of St Edith; ff. 263r-264v Wilton chronicle; ff. 265r-279v Life of St Æthelthryth
evidence.summary: Current component description supporting the first-quarter-fifteenth-century date, Wilton origin, Middle English verse life locus, adjacent Latin Wilton text, companion Æthelthryth life, and bibliographic history.
notes: The component still carries a legacy statement that digital images are unavailable, while the parent record now provides working full IIIF coverage. The lineage manifest preserves this conflict explicitly.
Its bibliography prints Black's edition year as 1802; the inspected edition history and DIMEV support 1830.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the British Library IIIF sequence and complete local bundle for Cotton MS Faustina B III.
evidence.locator: poem current ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535; encoded Latin selection current ff. 263r-264r / canvases 535-537; complete adjacent Latin texts through f. 264v / canvas 538
evidence.summary: Confirms the current work boundary and five-folio offset from Horstmann's milestones. Canvas 535/f. 263r carries the poem ending above and Nomina fundatorum below; canvas 537/f. 264r contains the end of Nomina and the first seven Auctoritates entries retained by CME00147. Canvas 538/f. 264v continues Auctoritates but is not encoded.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 421, 'Almighty God in Trinity,' version 1.0.
evidence.summary: Identifies the poem as The Life of St Editha, supplies incipit and explicit, records the sole witness at current ff. 199-263, and cites Black 1830 and Horstmann 1883.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached DIMEV v1.0 source release.

evidence.citation: Michael Benskin, 'In reply to Dr Burton,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 22 (1991): 209-262.
evidence.locator: especially pp. 246-247 and the Part III analysis
evidence.summary: Distinguishes the South Wiltshire language of likely originals from the mixed, predominantly West Midland language of the extant copy; associates the scribe with northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and probably Wilton; confirms that the same hand copied the Edith poem, Latin account, and Æthelthryth life; and rejects simple autograph and author-dialect assumptions.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached 55-page article PDF.

evidence.citation: Brepols, Mary Dockray-Miller, Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience (2009).
evidence.summary: Official publisher description for the modern edition and translation, conventional title The Wilton Chronicle, Wilton and early-fifteenth-century composition, female religious audience, subject framing, bibliographic details, and current purchase access.

evidence.citation: Mechthild Gretsch, review of Saints Edith and Æthelthryth, English Historical Review 126, no. 518 (2011): 121-122.
evidence.summary: Independent scholarly review describing the historical opening, the life after the manuscript lacuna, likely first-quarter-fifteenth-century composition, possible common authorship with the Æthelthryth poem, close Wilton connection, and the manuscript's probable production at Wilton around 1425.

evidence.citation: The Medieval Review, review of Mary Dockray-Miller, Saints Edith and Æthelthryth (2009).
evidence.summary: Summarizes the modern edition and its argument that the Wilton poet primarily adapted the eleventh-century Goscelin-associated Legenda Edithae to educate and entertain religious and lay women, promote Wilton, and combine history with hagiography.

evidence.citation: Cynthia Turner Camp, 'Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History,' in Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England (Boydell & Brewer, 2015), 25-63.
evidence.summary: Scholarly chapter abstract framing the poem as an anonymous work composed around 1420 for an identifiable female community, reconstructing Wilton's pre-Conquest past through joined hagiographic and historical writing.

open_questions.question: Was the anonymous Wilton poet a chaplain, another male cleric, a nun, or another member of the abbey's textual community?
next_steps: Retain anonymous authorship unless documentary or paleographic evidence identifies a person.
Consult the full argument and notes in Dockray-Miller and later scholarship for any post-2009 reassessment of gender or office.
notes: Close institutional affiliation is well supported; personal identity and profession are not.

open_questions.question: Which recension or physical exemplar of the Legenda sanctae Edithae underlies the poem, and how exactly is it related to the short Goscelin-associated Vita printed by the Bollandists?
next_steps: Compare Dockray-Miller's source notes against Horstmann's objections and current Goscelin scholarship.
Do not convert the Auctoritates list into a claim for an identified physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: What was the exact text and line count of the twelve-folio gathering lost after surviving line 1030?
next_steps: Compare source analogues and later summaries without presenting reconstruction as surviving text.
Seek physical or codicological evidence for when the gathering was removed.
notes: Horstmann's approximately 980 missing lines is an estimate and is excluded from the surviving verse count.

open_questions.question: Which manuscript features does each of Horstmann's italicized letter sequence represent: abbreviation expansion, supplied text, correction, overwritten reading, or another editorial distinction?
next_steps: Collate CME00147's 6,738 italic HI spans against the complete IIIF manuscript images and the 1883 page images.
Locate an explicit typographic key in Horstmann's broader editorial practice or correspondence before assigning one function globally.
notes: The facsimile now makes this comparison possible; the manifest deliberately does not equate all italics with one kind of elision or expansion.
The U-M catalog places St Edith in an author position, but she is the poem's subject rather than its author; that catalog convenience is not transferred to responsibility metadata.
The preferred title follows current scholarship. Chronicon Vilodunense and The Life of St Edith remain searchable alternates.
The poem is one work in one witness. The three LG wrappers are representation segments, not independent works.

The Latin addendum is medieval companion content in the same hand and is included because CME00147 encodes all of Nomina fundatorum and the opening half of Auctoritates. It remains separately scoped from the English verse work, and the omitted second half of Auctoritates is not implied by the part record.
The surviving manuscript copy is not treated as autograph. Benskin's dialect analysis requires separating likely original language, scribal language, place of composition, and place of copying.
Current foliation is used for discovery and facsimile navigation; Horstmann's old foliation is retained for reproducibility of his edition and the XML milestones.
A complete British Library IIIF bundle, not merely the target folios, is cached in the Git-ignored source-cache directory and linked through the lineage manifest.
